producedFindings.title: Stale manager reference not cleared on re-enablement
producedFindings.snippet: // When the same renderer type re-enables (e.g., circuit restart or new circuit on same page),
producedFindings.description: When a new circuit starts (same renderer ID), the old manager reference is overwritten without being disposed. The comment states that disposing would cause JSDisconnectedException, but the old manager object may still be referenced elsewhere, leading to potential use-after-free or incorrect behavior if the old manager is used.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider setting the old manager to undefined or marking it as invalid before assigning the new manager. Ensure any pending operations using the old manager are canceled or handled gracefully.

producedFindings.title: Missing null check for jsComponentParameters in enableJSRootComponents
producedFindings.description: The code assumes jsComponentParameters[componentIdentifier] exists, but jsComponentParameters is not stored globally anymore (only passed during enableJSRootComponents). If jsComponentParameters is not saved and the initializer is called later (e.g., after a re-enablement), it may access undefined. However, the current flow initializes only once due to hasInitializedJsComponents flag, so risk is low.
producedFindings.recommendation: Store jsComponentParameters globally (like before) or ensure jsComponentParameters is always available when initializers are called. Alternatively, add a null check.

producedFindings.title: Potential race condition during re-enablement
producedFindings.description: The check for duplicate enablement only triggers when manager exists and rendererId matches. If a new circuit starts while the old one is still active (unlikely but possible), the condition may not catch it, leading to two managers for the same renderer ID. This could cause undefined behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a more robust state management, perhaps by ensuring that only one manager per rendererId can exist at any time, and clear the manager when the circuit disconnects.

producedFindings.title: Deleted test file not fully integrated
producedFindings.description: The test file StatePersistanceJSRootTest.cs was deleted, and its test cases were moved to StatePersistenceTest.cs. However, the test setup (serverFixture.AdditionalArguments) is removed, and the new test relies on the default registration of the JS root component. This change may affect test isolation or configuration if the default registration interferes with other tests.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure the test does not depend on global state left by other tests. Consider adding a comment explaining why the registration is now always enabled.

producedFindings.title: Unused variable jsComponentParametersByIdentifier
producedFindings.description: The variable jsComponentParametersByIdentifier is declared but never assigned or used after the changes. It should be removed to avoid confusion and dead code.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the unused variable declaration.

groundTruth.category: Use Curly Braces for All Control Flow Statements
groundTruth.title: Missing braces in for loop
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all control flow statements (if, else, for, foreach, while, do-while) to use curly braces even for single-line bodies, but the inner for loop omits curly braces

groundTruth.title: Inverted renderer type check prevents circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The condition checking for different renderer types has been inverted from `!==` to `===`. This causes the function to throw an error when the SAME renderer type tries to re-enable JS root components (e.g., during circuit restart), which is the exact scenario that should be allowed. The original intent was to throw an error only when a DIFFERENT renderer type attempts to enable root components. This bug breaks circuit restart functionality, causing applications to fail when users reconnect after a circuit disconnect.

groundTruth.title: Missing parameter update causes stale data on circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The assignment `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` has been removed from the re-enabling logic. When a circuit restarts and `enableJSRootComponents` is called again with new parameters, the global `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` is not updated. This causes the application to continue using stale parameters from the previous circuit. The `DynamicRootComponent` class constructor and methods rely on `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` to retrieve component parameter definitions, so they will operate with outdated metadata after a circuit restart.

producedFindings.title: Potential race condition in hasInitializedJsComponents
producedFindings.description: The flag `hasInitializedJsComponents` is set to true after initializing JS components, but if `enableJSRootComponents` is called concurrently from multiple renderers (e.g., during rapid circuit restarts), there's a risk that initialization could happen multiple times before the flag is set, leading to duplicate calls to initializer functions. This could cause unintended side effects like registering custom elements twice.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure atomicity of the initialization check and set operation, or document that concurrent calls are not supported. Since this is likely called only during startup, the risk is low, but adding a guard like a lock or ensuring the flag is set before iterating could be considered.

producedFindings.title: Missing disposal of old manager reference
producedFindings.snippet: // When the same renderer type re-enables (e.g., circuit restart or new circuit on same page),
producedFindings.description: The comment mentions that the old manager's DotNetObjectReference is no longer valid and should not be disposed to avoid JSDisconnectedException. However, the old manager reference is overwritten without any cleanup, which could lead to a memory leak if the old manager holds resources. While the circuit is gone, the JavaScript object might persist.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider explicitly setting the old manager to undefined or null after overwriting, or document that the garbage collector will handle it. Since the old circuit is gone, this is likely safe, but clarity would help.

producedFindings.title: Inconsistent error message for multi-host scenario
producedFindings.snippet: throw new Error('Dynamic root components have already been enabled.');
producedFindings.description: The error message says 'Dynamic root components have already been enabled.' but the condition checks if `manager` exists and `currentRendererId === rendererId`. This implies the error is thrown only when the same renderer tries to enable again, which contradicts the comment about multi-host scenarios. The message should be updated to reflect the actual condition.
producedFindings.recommendation: Update the error message to something like 'Dynamic root components have already been enabled for this renderer.' to match the logic, or adjust the condition if the intent is to block multiple renderer types.
